reasoning: Both models skipped the only file presented (PKGBUILD) because the tool misclassified it as a 'binary file' and aborted the initial PKGBUILD audit. No files were actually reviewed by either model, so no security conclusion can be drawn. The verdict is inconclusive due to a tooling failure, not a property of the package itself.
disagreement_analysis: There is no disagreement — both models produced identical 'skipped' results for the same reason: the initial PKGBUILD audit failed with a 'Skipping binary file: PKGBUILD' error, causing both to abort before reviewing any content.
coverage_issues: PKGBUILD was never reviewed by any model — the single most important file for an AUR audit was skipped
No .install scripts, source URLs, checksums, or build/package functions were inspected
The binary-file misdetection short-circuited the entire audit pipeline
false_positives: The 'binary file: PKGBUILD' classification is itself a false positive of the file-type detection heuristic — a PKGBUILD is a plain-text shell script and should never be flagged as binary
re_audit_focus: PKGBUILD — verify source URLs (likely JetBrains EAP download), sha256sums/integrity, and build/package functions
Confirm the file encoding/content that triggered the binary-file misdetection (e.g., non-UTF-8 bytes, BOM, or unusual line endings)
learnings: The binary-file detection heuristic is incorrectly flagging plain-text PKGBUILDs as binary, aborting audits entirely; this detection should whitelist known text files (PKGBUILD, .install, .sh) or use more robust text/binary heuristics tolerant of non-ASCII bytes

A failure to read the PKGBUILD should not silently produce a 'skipped/no issues found' style output — it should surface as a hard error requiring manual review, which it partially does, but the dual 'No issues found' messaging is misleading
Consider a fallback: if the primary text-detection fails, attempt a forced UTF-8/latin-1 read of PKGBUILD since it is contractually a shell script
When all models skip due to the same tooling error, the orchestrator should auto-flag for re-audit rather than treating it as a model-quality issue
Investigate whether non-UTF-8 characters (e.g., accented contributor names in comments, common in JetBrains-related AUR packages) are tripping the binary detection
